title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the lighthouse. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Guardians of the Coast: The Evolution of Lighthouses
content.text: For as long as humans have ventured onto the sea, they have needed a way to find their way back to land. The earliest lighthouses were simple wood fires built on hilltops to guide local fishermen. However, as maritime trade expanded, so did the need for more permanent and powerful signals. ||(41)|| [41]This ancient wonder used a massive fire at its summit, reflected by bronze mirrors, to cast a beam that could be seen for miles[/41]. It stood for over a thousand years before being destroyed by earthquakes, setting a standard for coastal architecture that would not be matched until the Renaissance. ||(42)|| [42]These medieval towers were often managed by monks who viewed the maintenance of the light as a religious duty[/42]. However, as the Age of Discovery began, the responsibility shifted to the state. The challenge for engineers was how to build a structure that could withstand the direct, relentless battering of the Atlantic waves. ||(43)|| [43]Smeaton’s innovation was to use interlocking stone blocks, inspired by the trunk of an oak tree, which gave the tower the flexibility and strength to absorb the impact of the sea[/43]. This 'dovetail' masonry became the blueprint for all future wave-swept lighthouses. ||(44)|| [44]While the structures were now solid, the light itself remained weak, usually provided by candles or coal fires[/44]. The breakthrough came in the 1820s with Augustin-Jean Fresnel, a French physicist who developed a revolutionary lens. ||(45)|| [45]This 'Fresnel lens' allowed lighthouses to project a beam that was far more powerful than anything previously imagined[/45]. It turned the lighthouse into a precision instrument, capable of saving thousands of lives by marking dangerous reefs and sandbars with surgical accuracy. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the lighthouse keeper has largely been replaced by automated sensors and satellite navigation[/46]. Many of these historic towers have been decommissioned, their rotating beams replaced by the silent pings of GPS. Yet, they remain powerful symbols of human resilience and our eternal struggle to tame the wild uncertainty of the ocean.

paragraphs.D: Indeed, [41] the first true masterpiece [/41] of this kind was the Pharos of Alexandria, built in the 3rd century BC. Standing over 100 meters tall, it was considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
paragraphs.G: This [42] architectural legacy [/42] was slowly rediscovered as trade routes reopened across Europe. Merchant guilds began funding the construction of stone towers at the entrances to major ports, ensuring that their valuable cargoes of spices and silk could arrive safely after dark.
paragraphs.E: This [43] engineering problem [/43] was famously solved by John Smeaton in 1759 with the construction of the third Eddystone Lighthouse. Located on a treacherous reef off the coast of Devon, the site had already claimed two previous towers.
paragraphs.F: The [44] success of this design [/44] was a turning point in maritime safety. It proved that it was possible to plant a permanent light in the most hostile environments, extending the reach of the navy and the merchant fleet into previously 'unnavigable' waters.
paragraphs.C: By [45] using a series of glass prisms [/45] arranged in a beehive shape, he found a way to capture and concentrate nearly all the light from a central lamp into a single, horizontal beam.
paragraphs.B: This [46] transition to automation [/46] began in the mid-20th century. Solar panels and electric bulbs removed the need for someone to constantly trim wicks and polish mirrors, leading to the end of a unique and solitary profession that had existed for centuries.
paragraphs.A: Many [47] modern tourists [/47] now seek out these remote towers as unique vacation rentals. They are drawn to the rugged isolation and the historical charm of the buildings, providing a new source of revenue for the preservation of coastal heritage.

questions.explanation: D introduces the Pharos of Alexandria, which directly fits the description of the 'ancient wonder' mentioned after the gap.
G links the 'architectural legacy' of the ancient world to the medieval towers and merchant guilds mentioned in the text.
E identifies the 'engineering problem' (withstanding waves) and introduces John Smeaton and the Eddystone Lighthouse.
F refers to the 'success of this design' (Smeaton’s dovetail masonry) and its impact on maritime safety.
C explains how the Fresnel lens worked using 'glass prisms', following the mention of Fresnel in the text.
B describes the 'transition to automation' and the end of the 'lighthouse keeper' role mentioned in the final paragraph.
